summary:
Neurological Emergencies

Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)

Stroke is a sudden disruption of blood flow to the brain, causing neurological deficits. It is a time-critical emergency — "time is brain." There are two main types:

  • Ischemic stroke (~87%): A thrombus or embolus occludes a cerebral artery. Onset may be gradual (thrombotic) or sudden (embolic). Treatment: thrombolytics (tPA) within a narrow window (typically 0-4.5 hours from symptom onset).
  • Hemorrhagic stroke (~13%): A cerebral blood vessel ruptures, causing bleeding into the brain tissue or subarachnoid space. Associated with hypertension, aneurysm, or AV malformation. Presents with a sudden severe headache ("thunderclap"), vomiting, rapid deterioration, and higher mortality.

Stroke screening tools:

  • Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale (CPSS): Facial droop (have the patient smile), arm drift (close eyes, hold arms out for 10 seconds), speech (repeat a simple phrase). Any one abnormal finding = high probability of stroke.
  • FAST: Face, Arms, Speech, Time — same as CPSS with added emphasis on time of onset.
  • BEFAST: Balance (sudden dizziness/loss of coordination), Eyes (sudden vision loss/diplopia), Face (droop), Arms (weakness), Speech (slurred/difficulty), Time — expands FAST to detect posterior circulation strokes.
Clinical Pearl: Document the exact time the patient was LAST KNOWN WELL (not just when symptoms were discovered). This determines eligibility for thrombolytic therapy. If the patient woke up with symptoms, the last known well time is when they went to bed.

Seizures

A seizure is a transient episode of abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Seizure types include:

  • Tonic-clonic (grand mal): Generalized stiffening (tonic phase, 10-30 seconds) followed by rhythmic jerking (clonic phase, 1-3 minutes). Loss of consciousness, possible cyanosis, incontinence, and tongue biting. Postictal confusion follows.
  • Absence (petit mal): Brief (5-10 seconds) loss of awareness with staring, no convulsions. Common in children. May appear as daydreaming. No postictal state.
  • Focal (partial) seizures: Originate in one area of the brain. Simple focal: no loss of consciousness (e.g., jerking of one limb). Complex focal: altered awareness with automatisms (lip smacking, picking at clothes).

Status Epilepticus

Status epilepticus is defined as a single seizure lasting >5 minutes or two or more seizures without a return to baseline consciousness in between. It is a life-threatening medical emergency — prolonged seizure activity can cause brain damage, rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, and respiratory failure. EMS provider management:

  • Protect the airway — position the patient on their side (recovery position) if possible
  • Do NOT restrain the patient or put anything in their mouth
  • Clear the area of hazards
  • Administer oxygen and monitor SpO₂
  • Check blood glucose — treat hypoglycemia if present
  • Request ALS for medication (benzodiazepines: diazepam, midazolam, lorazepam)
  • Expedite transport

Seizure Precautions

  • DO protect the head with padding
  • DO time the seizure duration
  • DO loosen tight clothing
  • DO stay with the patient until the seizure ends
  • DO NOT restrain the patient
  • DO NOT place anything in the mouth (including oral airways, tongue blades, or your fingers)
  • DO NOT attempt to hold the tongue — it cannot be swallowed
  • DO NOT give the patient anything by mouth during or immediately after the seizure

Postictal State

The postictal state is the recovery period following a seizure. It can last minutes to hours and is characterized by confusion, drowsiness, headache, nausea, transient focal deficits (Todd's paralysis — temporary weakness on one side), and amnesia for the event. Management: monitor airway and mental status, reorient the patient, check blood glucose, and assess for injury. Transport for evaluation of first-time seizures, prolonged postictal state, or any complication.

Altered Mental Status — The Differential (AEIOU-TIPS)

When confronted with a patient with altered mental status, use the AEIOU-TIPS mnemonic to systematically consider causes:

LetterCauses
AAlcohol, Abuse (intoxication/withdrawal)
EEpilepsy (seizure/postictal), Electrolytes (imbalance)
IInsulin (hypo/hyperglycemia) — always check blood glucose!
OOverdose, Oxygen (hypoxia — check SpO₂)
UUremia (kidney failure), Underdose (missed medications)
TTrauma (head injury, intracranial bleed), Temperature (hyper/hypothermia), Tumor
IInfection (meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis)
PPsychiatric (acute psychosis, catatonia), Poison
SStroke (ischemic/hemorrhagic), Shock (hypoperfusion), Subarachnoid hemorrhage, SAH

Meningitis

Meningitis is inflammation of the meninges (the protective layers surrounding the brain and spinal cord), usually caused by bacterial or viral infection. Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency. Key signs include:

  • Fever — Often high-grade
  • Nuchal rigidity — Neck stiffness and pain with flexion (cannot touch chin to chest)
  • Severe headache — Often described as the worst headache of life
  • Altered mental status — Confusion, lethargy, or irritability
  • Photophobia — Sensitivity to light
  • Petechial or purpuric rash — Especially in meningococcal meningitis (Neisseria meningitidis); a non-blanching rash is an ominous sign
  • Kernig sign — Pain with knee extension when the hip is flexed to 90 degrees
  • Brudzinski sign — Involuntary hip/knee flexion when the neck is flexed supine

EMS provider management: rapid transport, droplet precautions (mask the patient and yourself), supportive care, and monitoring for deterioration.

Hypoglycemia and Altered Mental Status

Hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) is a common and reversible cause of altered mental status. It can mimic stroke, seizure, or intoxication. Always check blood glucose in any patient with altered mental status, focal neurological deficits, or seizure activity. Signs include diaphoresis, tachycardia, tremors, confusion, combativeness, or unconsciousness. EMS provider management: if the patient is awake and can swallow, administer oral glucose. If unconscious or unable to swallow, administer glucagon IM (per protocol) and transport.

quiz.q: An EMS provider uses the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale to assess a patient. Which three findings are evaluated?
options: Facial droop, arm drift, and speech abnormalities
Grip strength, pupil reactivity, and gait
Blood pressure, heart rate, and respiratory rate
quiz.explanation: The Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale (CPSS) assesses facial droop, arm drift, and speech. BEFAST adds Balance and Eyes for posterior circulation strokes.

quiz.q: A patient is found unresponsive with rhythmic jerking of all four limbs. A witness states the jerking has been continuous for approximately 6 minutes. What is this condition called?
quiz.explanation: A seizure lasting >5 minutes defines status epilepticus — a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate intervention.

quiz.q: A 65-year-old male with diabetes is found confused and diaphoretic. He has slurred speech and right-sided arm weakness. His blood glucose is 48 mg/dL. After administering oral glucose (he is able to swallow), his symptoms completely resolve. What does this presentation most likely represent?
options: An ischemic stroke that resolved spontaneously
Hypoglycemia mimicking a stroke — the glucose corrected the neurological deficits
A transient ischemic attack (TIA) unrelated to his blood sugar
A complex partial seizure with postictal weakness
quiz.explanation: Hypoglycemia can mimic stroke with focal neurological deficits. Always check blood glucose in any patient with stroke-like symptoms — correction of hypoglycemia may resolve the deficits.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke?
flashcards.answer: Ischemic stroke (~87%) is caused by a clot obstructing a cerebral artery. Hemorrhagic stroke (~13%) is caused by a ruptured vessel. Hemorrhagic stroke typically presents with a sudden severe headache ('thunderclap'), vomiting, and more rapid deterioration.

flashcards.question: What does BEFAST stand for and how does it differ from FAST?
flashcards.answer: BEFAST: Balance (sudden dizziness/ataxia), Eyes (sudden vision loss/diplopia), Face (droop), Arm (weakness), Speech (slurred), Time. It adds Balance and Eyes to FAST to detect posterior circulation strokes.

flashcards.question: Describe the phases of a tonic-clonic seizure.
flashcards.answer: Tonic phase (10-30 sec): stiffening of all muscles, loss of consciousness, possible cyanosis. Clonic phase (1-3 min): rhythmic jerking, incontinence, tongue biting. Postictal phase: confusion, drowsiness, headache, amnesia for the event.

flashcards.question: What is status epilepticus and why is it an emergency?
flashcards.answer: A single seizure lasting >5 minutes or multiple seizures without return to baseline. It is life-threatening because prolonged seizure activity can cause brain damage, rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, and respiratory failure.

flashcards.question: What are the key seizure precautions (DOs and DON'Ts)?
flashcards.answer: DO: protect the head, time the seizure, loosen clothing, stay with the patient. DO NOT: restrain, put anything in the mouth, hold the tongue, or give anything by mouth during/seizing.

flashcards.question: What does the AEIOU-TIPS mnemonic help you remember?
flashcards.answer: Causes of altered mental status: Alcohol, Epilepsy/Electrolytes, Insulin (glucose), Overdose/Oxygen, Uremia, Trauma/Temperature/Tumor, Infection, Psychiatric/Poison, Stroke/Shock/SAH.

flashcards.question: What are the classic signs of bacterial meningitis?
flashcards.answer: Fever, nuchal rigidity (neck stiffness), severe headache, altered mental status, photophobia, and petechial/purpuric rash (meningococcal). Kernig and Brudzinski signs may be positive.

flashcards.question: How should an EMS provider manage a hypoglycemic patient with altered mental status who is unconscious?
flashcards.answer: Ensure airway patency, check blood glucose (<70 mg/dL confirms), administer glucagon IM (per protocol), position in recovery position, monitor for seizure activity, and transport. Do NOT attempt oral glucose in an unconscious patient.

critical.scenario: You are called to a 72-year-old female whose daughter found her on the living room floor 30 minutes ago. The patient is confused, has slurred speech, and cannot lift her left arm. Her left facial droop is obvious. She is able to follow simple commands with her right side. Blood glucose is 112 mg/dL. The daughter reports the patient was fine at breakfast 2 hours ago and has no history of seizures or diabetes. She takes medication for hypertension.
critical.question: Given the acute onset of unilateral weakness, facial droop, and speech deficit with a negative hypoglycemia screen, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the critical information you need to document?

options.t: Bell's palsy — reassure and transport non-emergently
options.f: Incorrect. Bell's palsy affects only the face (not arm/speech) and has a different onset pattern. This is a stroke until proven otherwise.

options.t: Acute ischemic stroke — document the exact time the patient was LAST KNOWN WELL (2 hours ago at breakfast), perform BEFAST assessment, place in position of comfort, give oxygen if SpO₂ <94%, and transport rapidly to a stroke center
options.f: Correct. Time of last known well determines thrombolytic eligibility. Rapid transport to a stroke-capable center is critical.

critical.explanation: Every minute counts in stroke care. Document last known well time precisely — this determines eligibility for tPA. Transport to the highest-level stroke center available.

critical.scenario: You respond to a 30-year-old male who had a seizure at work. Coworkers report he suddenly stiffened, fell to the ground, and began jerking rhythmically for approximately 3 minutes. He is now confused, drowsy, and does not know where he is. He has a small laceration on his tongue. His vital signs are stable and his blood glucose is 96 mg/dL. He has no prior history of seizures according to his coworkers.
critical.question: The patient is now in the postictal phase. What are your management priorities for this first-time seizure?

options.t: The seizure has ended and he is stable — no transport needed; have him follow up with his primary care doctor
options.f: Incorrect. First-time seizures require emergency department evaluation to rule out serious underlying causes (tumor, bleed, infection, metabolic).

options.t: Monitor airway and mental status, provide reassurance, assess for injury (tongue laceration), check blood glucose, and transport for medical evaluation of this first-time seizure
options.f: Correct. First-time seizures require ED evaluation. Continue monitoring for recurrent seizure activity during transport.

critical.explanation: Any first-time seizure warrants ED evaluation to identify the cause (tumor, bleed, infection, metabolic). Protect the airway, monitor closely for recurrent seizure, and transport.

critical.scenario: You are called to a college dormitory for a 19-year-old female with fever and confusion. Her roommate reports she has had a severe headache, fever, and has been vomiting for 24 hours. On exam, the patient is confused and irritable. Her temperature is 103.2°F. When you ask her to touch her chin to her chest, she cannot do so without severe pain. As you examine her further, you notice a non-blanching petechial rash on her trunk and lower extremities.
critical.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and what precautions should you take?

options.t: Viral upper respiratory infection — provide supportive care and transport routinely
options.f: Incorrect. Fever + nuchal rigidity + petechial rash + altered mental status = meningococcal meningitis until proven otherwise.

options.t: Bacterial meningitis (meningococcal) — place a mask on the patient, wear a mask yourself (droplet precautions), provide oxygen, monitor for deterioration, and transport rapidly
options.f: Correct. Meningococcal meningitis is highly contagious via droplets and can progress rapidly. Apply droplet precautions and expedite transport.

critical.explanation: Bacterial meningitis, especially meningococcal, is a medical emergency requiring immediate antibiotics and droplet precautions. The classic triad is fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status; a petechial rash is an ominous sign.
